Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Understand the Goal The objective is to analyze an event log event_log to find the most common process variants sequences of activities per case identify the top K variants by frequency and then retrieve all original events belonging only to cases that follow those top K variants

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: Analyze the Schema Table event_log Columns case_id activity timestamp

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Step 1 Construct the Sequence per Case I need to order events by timestamp within each case_id I need to concatenate these activities into a single string or array representing the variant

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - IND
Text: DuckDB has string aggregation functions list_value list_concat list_join list_join is perfect for creating a readable sequence string

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Step 2 Group and Count Variants Now I have case_id and variant I need to count how many case_ids exist for each variant

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Step 3 Identify Top K Variants I need to sort by case_count descending and limit to K

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: Lets define K as a variable or parameter In DuckDB I can use a WITH clause or a CTE to define K Lets assume K equals 5 for the example or make it a parameter

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Step 4 Filter Original Events I need to join the original event_log with the list of case_ids that belong to the Top K variants

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Wait Step 1 gave me case_id to variant Step 2 and 3 gave me variant to Top K So I need to find which case_ids produced the Top K variants

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: Using Common Table Expressions CTEs makes the logic readable

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Correction I need the case_ids associated with these variants to filter the original log If I limit here I lose the case_ids Better approach Calculate counts first then select the top variants then join back to get case_ids then join to event_log

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Revised Plan 1 case_variants Map case_id to variant_sequence 2 variant_frequencies Count case_ids per variant_sequence 3 top_k_variants Select top variant_sequences from frequencies 4 top_k_cases Select case_ids from case_variants where variant_sequence is in top_k_variants 5 final_result Select from event_log where case_id is in top_k_cases

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: Wait simpler join 1 case_variants Map case_id to variant_sequence 2 variant_counts Aggregate case_variants to get counts per variant 3 top_k_variants Select top variants from variant_counts 4 Join event_log to case_variants to top_k_variants

Name: Validation - IND
Text: Addressing Potential Edge Cases Timestamps Sorting by timestamp is crucial Nulls If activity or timestamp is null list_join might behave unexpectedly Ties If multiple variants have the same count at the cutoff K LIMIT might behave arbitrarily Performance Grouping by case_id first is necessary

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Constructing the Final Query WITH case_variants AS SELECT case_id list_join activity ORDER BY timestamp AS variant FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id variant_counts AS SELECT variant COUNT AS frequency FROM case_variants GROUP BY variant ORDER BY frequency DESC LIMIT 5 valid_cases AS SELECT DISTINCT cv case_id FROM case_variants cv JOIN variant_counts vc ON cv variant equals vc variant SELECT el FROM event_log el JOIN valid_cases vc ON el case_id equals vc case_id ORDER BY el case_id el timestamp

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Review against Constraints Correctly extract ordered sequence Aggregate and count cases Filter down to top K Return corresponding events

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Self Correction on list_join In some DuckDB versions list_join takes an array and a separator The most robust standard way in DuckDB is list_join list_agg activity ORDER BY timestamp then separator

Name: Validation - PE
Text: DuckDB documentation says list_join list separator It is not an aggregate function It operates on a list So I must use list_agg aggregate to create the list then list_join function to stringify it Syntax list_join list_agg activity ORDER BY timestamp then separator This is the correct robust DuckDB way

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Refining the Plan 1 case_variants list_join list_agg activity ORDER BY timestamp 2 variant_counts Count distinct case_ids per variant 3 top_k Limit 4 Join back

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Final check on logic event_log to case_variants Case ID plus Sequence String case_variants to variant_counts Sequence String plus Count variant_counts to top_k_variants Top K Sequence Strings case_variants plus top_k_variants to valid_case_ids event_log plus valid_case_ids to Final Result This looks solid

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Actually CTE 4 is redundant if I join case_sequences directly with top_k_variants This avoids an extra CTE

Name: Validation - PE
Text: One more thing case_id uniqueness COUNT in variant_counts counts rows in case_variants Since case_variants is grouped by case_id each row is unique case_id So COUNT is equivalent to COUNT DISTINCT case_id
